title: Trees & BST
description: Build the intuition and code patterns for DFS-based tree problems, BST operations, and Lowest Common Ancestor — the toolkit behind dozens of Codeforces tree problems.

sections.content: 🌳 Trees in Competitive Programming

A tree with `n` nodes has exactly `n-1` edges, is connected, and contains no cycles. In contests, trees are almost always given as an **undirected adjacency list** with a designated root (often node 1). The primary tool is **DFS**, which reveals properties like depths, subtree sizes, heights, and paths. BSTs add an ordering constraint that enables binary-search-style queries.

sections.label: Rooted vs Unrooted
sections.content: Always root the tree at node 1 (or whatever the problem says) before DFS. Pass `parent` to avoid revisiting the edge you came from — this is simpler than a visited array for trees. `dfs(node, parent)` is the standard template.
📐 Core DFS Tree Template

Most tree DFS problems are solved by computing something for each node using its children's results (post-order), or propagating something down from parent to children (pre-order). Learn to identify which direction the information flows.

sections.title: Tree DFS Template — Depth, Size, Height

sections.content: 🔍 Key Tree DFS Patterns
📏 Tree Diameter

The **diameter** of a tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes. Two approaches: (1) Two-BFS: run BFS from any node, find the farthest node `u`, run BFS again from `u`, the farthest distance is the diameter. (2) DFS post-order: at each node, the diameter candidate is `height[left] + 1 + height[right] + 1` (the two longest branches combined).

sections.title: Tree Diameter — DFS and Two-BFS

sections.content: 🔢 Euler Tour (DFS In/Out Times)

Assign each node an **entry time** `tin[u]` and **exit time** `tout[u]` during DFS. This powerful technique lets you: (1) check if `v` is in the subtree of `u` with `tin[u] <= tin[v] && tout[v] <= tout[u]`, and (2) flatten subtree queries onto a linear array for range queries with Fenwick/Segment trees.

sections.title: Euler Tour — Subtree Flattening

sections.content: 👴 Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)

The **LCA** of nodes `u` and `v` is the deepest node that is an ancestor of both. LCA is used in path queries (`dist(u,v) = depth[u] + depth[v] - 2*depth[LCA(u,v)]`), tree DP, and many advanced problems. The **Binary Lifting** approach preprocesses in O(n log n) and answers each query in O(log n).

sections.title: LCA via Binary Lifting — O(n log n) preprocess, O(log n) query

sections.label: LCA Root Initialization
sections.content: Set `up[root][0] = root` (pointing to itself), NOT 0 or -1. This prevents index-out-of-bounds when lifting beyond the root, because `up[root][j]` will always resolve to `root` for all `j`. A parent of 0 causes `up[up[v][j-1]][j-1]` to access `up[0][...]` — undefined behavior.
🌲 Binary Search Tree (BST) Operations

A BST satisfies: all nodes in the left subtree of `u` have keys **less than** `u`, and all nodes in the right subtree have keys **greater than** `u`. This invariant enables O(log n) search, insert, and delete on balanced BSTs. In contests, **`std::set` and `std::map`** are balanced BSTs (Red-Black Trees) — prefer them over coding your own.

sections.title: BST: Manual Implementation with Insert, Search, Inorder

sections.content: 🔧 BST Properties & Validation

A common exam/contest question: **validate whether a binary tree is a valid BST**. The naive approach (left < root < right at each node) fails for deeper ancestors. The correct approach passes a valid range `[lo, hi]` down during DFS.

sections.title: BST Validation — Range-Propagation DFS

sections.label: 🚨 The #1 BST Validation Mistake
sections.content: Do NOT just check `node->left->val < node->val < node->right->val` locally. This misses cases like a node in the left subtree being greater than the root's ancestor. **Always propagate bounds**: pass `(lo, hi)` where every value in the subtree must satisfy `lo < val < hi`. Use `long long` bounds (LLONG_MIN / LLONG_MAX) to handle integer boundary values.
📊 Tree Problem Classification

rows: O(n log n + q log n)

sections.content: 🏋️ Practice Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Trace DFS Subtree Sizes on a Small Tree
sections.explanation:
Tree structure: 1 is root with children 2,3. Node 2 has children 4,5. Node 3 has child 6.
DFS visits (post-order to compute sizes):
  sz[4]=1 (leaf), sz[5]=1 (leaf) → sz[2]=1+1+1=3
  sz[6]=1 (leaf) → sz[3]=1+1=2
  sz[1]=1+3+2=6
Note that post-order means each node's size = 1 + sum of children's sizes, computed bottom-up.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Binary Lifting — Trace Ancestor Table
sections.explanation:
Chain is 1→2→3→4→5 from root to leaf. Depths: dep[1]=0, dep[2]=1, dep[3]=2, dep[4]=3, dep[5]=4.
For node 5:
  up[5][0] = parent = 4 (2^0=1 step up)
  up[5][1] = up[up[5][0]][0] = up[4][0] = 3 (2^1=2 steps up)
  up[5][2] = up[up[5][1]][1] = up[3][1] = up[up[3][0]][0] = up[2][0] = 1 (2^2=4 steps up)
So 4 steps up from node 5 reaches root node 1, correct since dep[5]=4.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Find the Diameter of a Weighted Tree
sections.approach: Use DFS post-order. At each node, track the two longest weighted paths going down into children. The diameter candidate at this node = best1 + best2. Return best1 (the longest single branch) to the parent. Update a global answer at each node.
sections.explanation: At each node we track the two deepest (heaviest) branches going into children. A path through node `u` uses the best + second-best branch = best1+best2. We update the global answer at every node and return best1 to the parent so it can extend the path further. Time: O(n). Space: O(n) recursion stack. This handles the diameter correctly even when it doesn't pass through the root. Edge case: n=1 → diameter=0 (no edges, ans stays 0).

sections.title: Exercise 4: Count Nodes in Subtree with Value in Range [L, R]
sections.approach:
1. Run Euler tour DFS to get tin[u] and tout[u] for each node.
2. Build array `flat[]` where `flat[tin[u]] = value[u]`.
3. For query (u, L, R): count values in flat[tin[u]..tout[u]] that are in [L,R].
4. For this solution, we use an offline approach: sort flat positions, answer queries using binary search on a sorted version of each range (merge sort tree / fractional cascading). For simplicity here, we demonstrate the Euler tour + per-query brute force (O(n) per query), noting the optimization.
sections.explanation: The Euler tour flattens the tree so that subtree(u) = contiguous range [tin[u], tout[u]] in the flat array. This transforms 'subtree queries' into 'range queries on a linear array'. The brute force query is O(n) per call, giving O(nq) total — too slow for full constraints. Optimization: use a **Merge Sort Tree** (vector of sorted arrays at each segment tree node) to answer range-count queries in O(log²n), giving O((n+q)log²n) total. For contests, **persistent segment tree on values** gives O(log n) per query after O(n log n) build.

sections.label: Iterative DFS for Deep Trees
sections.content: Recursive DFS hits stack overflow for n=10⁵ on a chain (linear tree). Always test your tree code on a bamboo (chain) input. If needed, convert to iterative DFS using an explicit stack, or increase stack size with `ulimit -s unlimited` (Linux only — not available in online judges). Iterative DFS is the safe default for trees with up to 10⁵ nodes.
⚡ Contest Checklist for Tree Problems

items: **Root the tree** before DFS — pass `(node, parent)` instead of using a visited array
**Watch for n=1** edge case — single node trees have no edges, diameter=0, LCA(1,1)=1
**Chain trees** cause O(n) recursion depth — test for stack overflow, use iterative DFS if needed
**LCA initialization**: set `up[root][0] = root` to avoid out-of-bounds in binary lifting
**Euler tour timer**: use a global `timer` variable, not a local one, so it increments across all DFS calls
**Weighted edges**: store as `vector<pair<int,int>> adj[]` (neighbor, weight), not plain `vector<int>`
**BST bounds**: use `long long` for validation bounds (LLONG_MIN/LLONG_MAX) to handle INT_MIN/INT_MAX node values
